mnemonics: GPS for Cost: Gradient Points Steepest (ascent/descent) for Quadratic Cost Functions
keyPoints: The gradient of a quadratic cost function, f(x) = (1/2)xᵀAx - bᵀx + c, is ∇f(x) = Ax - b.
Setting the gradient to zero (∇f(x) = 0) gives the system of linear equations (Ax = b) whose solution minimizes the cost.
The gradient's direction indicates the steepest ascent; moving in the opposite direction (-∇f(x)) is the method of steepest descent for minimization.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the negative sign in the gradient expression: writing ∇f(x) = b - Ax instead of Ax - b.
Mishandling the constant term 'c' and trying to take its derivative, even though it vanishes in the gradient.
Confusing the roles of matrix A and vector b, especially when the cost function is not written in standard quadratic form.
